Reuters reported on March 24 that, as per an executive at Broadcom, the company’s manufacturing partner, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. Ltd. (NYSE:TSM), is operating near full capacity as demand for AI chips continues to surge. The executive added that the broader industry is also facing supply chain constraints. He pointed out shortages in areas such as printed circuit boards and lasers, which have emerged as unexpected bottlenecks and are causing delays. To address these constraints, many customers are signing long-term agreements for 3-4 years. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. Ltd. (NYSE:TSM) has also said its customer planning timelines are now 2-3 years in advance due to tight capacity conditions. Director of product marketing in Broadcom’s Physical Layer Products division, Natarajan Ramachandran, commented: "We are seeing that TSMC is hitting (production capacity) limits. They will be ​increasing the capacity to 2027, but that has become a bottleneck, ⁠or that has kind of choked the supply chain in 2026." According to the CEO C.C. Wei, the company is working hard to expand the capacity in Arizona and Taiwan to meet demand. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. Ltd. (NYSE:TSM), which produces around 90% of the world’s most advanced chips, counts Nvidia and Apple among its key customers. Meanwhile, Samsung is also shifting towards three- to five-year long-term contracts to better manage industry cycles. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. Ltd. (NYSE:TSM) is the world’s largest semiconductor foundry and is engaged in the manufacturing and designing of semiconductor chips. These chips are used by companies across several end markets, including personal computers and peripheral products, consumer electronics, wired and wireless communications systems, and automotive and industrial equipment. title: ¡Lluvia, Pasión Y Fútbol En El TSM! 🌧⚽ | Santos Laguna Vs Toluca. [8b0a94] - fathomjournal.org
content:
Join over 9000 global opinion formers by subscribing to Fathom's influential Weekly Newsletter عيد الفطر 2026 متى — that's the question many are asking right now, especially those balancing cultural or family traditions with steady metabolic health goals. As of mid-March 2026, reliable sources including Saudi Arabia's official moon sighting announcement and astronomical calculations point to Friday, March 20, 2026 as the confirmed start of Eid al-Fitr, marking the end of Ramadan 1447 AH. Dates can shift slightly by region depending on local moon sighting committees, but March 20 is the widely accepted first day across much of the Muslim world and diaspora communities. For English-speaking readers in the US and Europe who prioritize evidence-based wellness, this timing matters. Eid arrives right after a month of intermittent fasting patterns, which can leave some feeling energized and others dealing with rebound hunger or energy dips when normal eating resumes. The festival itself centers on community meals, sweets, and gatherings — all of which can challenge the metabolic balance many work hard to maintain year-round. This article breaks down the date context, realistic ways to enjoy the celebration without derailing progress, and supplement options that some people use to smooth the transition. The focus stays practical: what actually helps adherence, where evidence is thin, and how to spot quality products amid the marketing noise. Eid al-Fitr, the Festival of Breaking the Fast, begins the day after Ramadan ends. In 2026, with Ramadan concluding on March 19 based on confirmed sightings, عيد الفطر 2026 متى resolves to March 20 as day one. Celebrations typically span three days, though customs vary — some families extend feasts through the weekend. This date lands on a Friday in many Western calendars, which can mean longer social eating windows for those in the US or Europe. People who already follow time-restricted eating or low-carb patterns often find the shift manageable because they've practiced hunger resilience during Ramadan. Others who maintain steady blood sugar through consistent meals may notice bigger swings when sweets and carb-heavy dishes appear suddenly. It fits best for folks already comfortable with metabolic flexibility — those who cycle carbs occasionally or use fasting windows strategically. If your routine relies on strict daily calorie control or you're deep into fat-adaptation, the sudden influx of dates, baklava, and rice dishes can feel disruptive. Short version: if you've handled holiday meals before without major setbacks, Eid 2026 is navigable. If post-fast rebounds have tripped you up in the past, planning ahead pays off. The biggest plus is social. Shared meals strengthen relationships, and taking part without guilt supports long-term consistency more than perfect isolation ever could. Many report better adherence the rest of the year after allowing controlled enjoyment during cultural events. Energy-wise, the end of prolonged daytime fasting often brings a noticeable lift in morning alertness for the first few days. Sleep patterns stabilize without pre-dawn meals, and hydration improves when water intake spreads evenly again. Where it falls short: portion creep. A single iftar-style plate can easily hit 800–1200 calories, mostly from carbs and fats together, which spikes insulin more than spaced-out eating. Sweets circulate constantly — one small piece leads to another for some. Digestive comfort takes a hit too; after reduced food volume for a month, heavy cream-based desserts or fried items can cause bloating or sluggishness. One client I worked with last year tried to "power through" with willpower alone. He loaded up on maamoul cookies at every visit, thinking he'd burn it off with extra walks. By day four he felt foggy, his morning ketone readings dropped sharply, and adherence slipped for two weeks after. The mistake wasn't the food — it was treating the entire three days like one extended cheat without any guardrails. Studies on Ramadan fasting show mixed but mostly neutral-to-positive short-term metabolic effects. A 2020 review in Nutrients noted improved insulin sensitivity in many participants, though results varied by baseline health and meal composition during non-fasting hours. The New England Journal of Medicine has covered intermittent fasting protocols that resemble Ramadan patterns, suggesting benefits for glucose regulation when total intake stays controlled. Post-Ramadan transition data is thinner. Small trials, including one from the Journal of Translational Medicine (2019), observed temporary weight regain and insulin spikes in the week after Eid due to overconsumption of refined carbs. Larger cohort studies from institutions like the Mayo Clinic on holiday eating patterns indicate that people who set loose boundaries (e.g., one mindful indulgence per day) regain less than unrestricted eaters. High-quality evidence has limits here. Most Ramadan studies run 4–8 weeks, use small samples (often under 100), and don't always control for supplement use or exact food choices. Funding sometimes comes from regional health bodies with cultural interest in positive framing. No large randomized trial specifically tracks metabolic markers across Eid al-Fitr 2026 or similar dates. Bottom line: fasting during Ramadan can support metabolic flexibility for many, but the refeed phase needs deliberate structure to avoid rebound issues. Supplements may help bridge the gap, but they don't replace mindful eating. Common supplements people reach for around Eid include electrolyte blends (to counter any lingering dehydration), fiber sources like psyllium or inulin (for satiety during rich meals), and exogenous ketones or MCT oils (for steady energy without carb reliance). Magnesium glycinate appears often for sleep and muscle relaxation after altered routines. Formats matter. Powders mix easily into water but taste chalky if unflavored. Capsules offer convenience but lower dosing flexibility. Gummies promise better compliance but usually carry 2–4 g added sugar per serving — counterproductive when trying to limit carbs. Quality signals: Look for GMP certification on the label, third-party testing results (NSF, USP, or ConsumerLab seals), and transparent sourcing (e.g., "C8 MCT from sustainable palm" vs vague "MCT oil"). Avoid products listing "proprietary blend" for key actives — that hides under-dosing. I tested a popular MCT C8 powder last Ramadan-to-Eid cycle. Texture was smooth, no grittiness, and it dissolved quickly in black coffee without an oily film. Taste stayed neutral — important when you're already full from dates. Energy felt even for about four hours, no crash. A competing brand with added flavorings left an artificial aftertaste and caused minor stomach upset in the first dose. Here's a straightforward comparison of five supplement categories people consider around Eid transitions. Doses reflect typical effective ranges from user reports and label realism. The fiber and electrolyte options tend to deliver the best day-to-day value for most people navigating feast periods. Start with your goal: satiety during meals, steady energy, or digestive ease? Match the product to that priority. Red flags: flashy before-after photos without disclaimers, "doctor formulated" without named credentials, or prices under $10 for 30 servings (usually means filler-heavy formulas). One frequent slip is starting a new supplement right on Eid day. A friend tried high-dose BHB ketones for the first time during a family gathering — ended up with stomach cramps halfway through dessert. The combination of rich food and untested tolerance didn't mix well. Better to trial 5–7 days before the holiday. Another: ignoring fiber ramp-up. Jumping straight to 10 g psyllium after low-fiber fasting often leads to gas. Start at 3–5 g a week prior. Over-relying on supplements instead of plate strategy also backfires. No powder fixes endless baklava portions. When exactly is عيد الفطر 2026 متى confirmed?
Friday, March 20, 2026, is the widely recognized first day based on Saudi Arabia's moon sighting and supporting astronomical data. Some regions may observe Saturday, March 21, if local committees differ. Can supplements really help during Eid meals?
They can support — electrolytes prevent headaches from dehydration shifts, fiber extends fullness, MCTs provide quick fuel. But they work best alongside portion awareness and walking after meals. Are gummies a good option for metabolic goals?
Usually not. Most contain enough added sugar to nudge blood glucose, which counters the point when you're already facing sweets. Capsules or powders give cleaner delivery. Who should skip certain supplements around this time?
Anyone with active reflux (MCTs can relax the LES), those on diabetes meds (exogenous ketones may affect insulin needs), or people prone to GI intolerance (high-fiber without slow introduction). How do I know if a product is worth the money?
Check for third-party testing, transparent dosing, and user reviews focused on real effects like satiety duration rather than hype. Trial small sizes first. Try this low-pressure approach starting a week before March 20: add one supplement (say, electrolytes morning and evening) while keeping your usual routine. Track morning energy, any bloating, and how hungry you feel mid-afternoon on a 1–10 scale. On Eid day, pick one or two favorite dishes mindfully, use the supplement as planned, and note how you feel the next morning. If energy stays even and digestion is comfortable, continue. If you notice crashes, GI issues, or no difference, stop — no need to force it. The goal isn't perfection; it's gathering data so next year's celebration feels easier. I specialize in testing supplements designed to support keto adherence and metabolic performance. Over the past five years, I’ve personally reviewed more than 80 consumer products, analyzing how they affect appetite control, daily consistency, digestive comfort, and long-term usability. My background in quality assurance and ingredient sourcing helps me evaluate formulation standards beyond surface-level claims. title: Will TSM's Aggressive Capex Plan Strengthen Its Foundry Dominance? - TradingView
content: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company TSM, also known as TSMC, is significantly increasing its capital spending to maintain its leadership in advanced chip manufacturing. The company plans to invest between $52 billion and $56 billion in 2026, indicating a 27-37% increase from about $40.9 billion in 2025. This aggressive spending reflects strong confidence in long-term demand, especially from artificial intelligence (AI) and high-performance computing markets. A large portion of this investment, around 70% to 80%, is directed toward advanced process technologies such as 3-nanometer and 2-nanometer nodes. These technologies are critical for AI accelerators and next-generation processors, where Taiwan Semiconductor already has a clear lead over competitors. By expanding capacity in these nodes, TSMC aims to stay ahead of rivals and meet growing customer demand. Taiwan Semiconductor is expanding its fabrication facilities across different countries. In the United States alone, TSM is investing $165 billion over the next few years to build five new state-of-the-art fabrication facilities and two advanced packaging facilities in Arizona. These facilities will boost the U.S. semiconductor supply chain for chips used in AI and high-performance computing. Apart from this, it is expanding fabrication facilities across Germany, Japan and Taiwan. This global expansion reflects Taiwan Semiconductor’s response to customer demand for geographic flexibility and government incentives. By locating capacity closer to key clients and end markets, the company strengthens its role as a critical supplier in the semiconductor supply chain. We believe that Taiwan Semiconductor’s global fab expansion push will help it capitalize on the growing opportunities from rising AI-related chip demand and meet its 2026 revenue growth target of approximately 30%. The Zacks Consensus Estimate for TSMC’s 2026 revenues is currently pegged at $160 billion, indicating year-over-year growth of 30.7%. Intel INTC and GlobalFoundries GFS are also expanding their presence in AI chip manufacturing. Intel is investing heavily in its foundry business, aiming to produce advanced chips. The company is currently focusing on its 18A process, which signifies 1.8nm chips. Intel’s 18A process is claimed to have higher performance and efficiency, which will help the company better compete with Taiwan Semiconductor’s upcoming N2 chips. GlobalFoundries focuses more on mature nodes. The company is witnessing some AI-related demand, especially in edge computing and embedded AI. GlobalFoundries is working to expand capacity in the United States and Europe to attract customers looking for supply-chain flexibility. TSM’s Share Price Performance, Valuation and Estimates Shares of Taiwan Semiconductor have surged around 95% over the past year compared with the Zacks Computer and Technology sector’s appreciation of 30.6%. Taiwan Semiconductor One-Year Price Return Performance From a valuation standpoint, TSM trades at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 21.55, lower than the sector’s average of 22.81. The Zacks Consensus Estimate for Taiwan Semiconductor’s 2026 and 2027 earnings implies a year-over-year increase of 34.9% and 22.9%, respectively. The consensus mark for 2026 and 2027 earnings has been revised upward over the past 30 days. Taiwan Semiconductor currently carries a Zacks Rank #3 (Hold). title: TSM Roofing Expands with New Phoenix Headquarters - National Today
content: Arizona's Owens Corning Platinum Preferred Roofing Contractor Moves to Larger Facility to Serve More Homeowners TSM Roofing LLC, a family-owned roofing contractor serving Phoenix and Prescott, Arizona, has announced the purchase of a new facility and the relocation of its headquarters to 2630 W Deer Valley Rd, Phoenix, AZ 85027 by April 30, 2026. The move will allow the company to scale operations, take on more projects, and deliver faster service to homeowners across the Phoenix metro and Prescott areas. The new headquarters represents a significant step in TSM Roofing's growth since its founding in 2016. With greater physical space, enhanced logistics, and increased operational capacity, the company will be better equipped to meet the unique roofing challenges faced by homeowners in Phoenix and Prescott, including intense heat, UV exposure, sudden storms, and temperature swings. The new 2630 W Deer Valley Rd facility will provide TSM Roofing with the room to expand its team and take on more projects simultaneously. This will allow the company to respond to service requests more quickly and continue delivering the quality craftsmanship it has become known for across both the Phoenix metro and Prescott areas. A family-owned and operated roofing contractor serving Phoenix, Prescott, and communities throughout Maricopa and Yavapai counties in Arizona. The company holds the Owens Corning Platinum Preferred designation, a distinction earned by fewer than one percent of roofing contractors nationally. “Purchasing this building is about more than a new address. It is a statement of where TSM Roofing is headed and the level of service we intend to deliver to every homeowner who trusts us with their roof. This space gives our team the room to grow the right way, so that our customers in Phoenix and Prescott continue to experience the reliability and quality they deserve.” The relocation to the new Phoenix headquarters at 2630 W Deer Valley Rd will be completed by April 30, 2026. TSM Roofing's expansion to a larger facility demonstrates the company's commitment to scaling its operations in a way that directly benefits homeowners in the Phoenix metro and Prescott areas, allowing it to take on more projects, respond to service requests faster, and continue delivering the quality craftsmanship it has become known for. Today's intraday share price movement for Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company was influenced by a confluence of factors, primarily stemming from geopolitical and macroeconomic uncertainties alongside shifts in institutional sentiment and emerging competitive dynamics. Geopolitical risks remain a significant overhang, with lingering concerns emanating from the Middle East contributing to investor caution across global markets. These tensions raise fears of potential supply chain disruptions and higher energy prices, which could particularly impact wafer fabrication centers like Taiwan due to their reliance on imported energy sources. Concurrently, ongoing geopolitical tensions between China and Taiwan consistently present a risk to the company, given its pivotal role in the global semiconductor industry and the potential for broader economic implications from any regional instability. Institutional portfolio adjustments also contributed to the downward pressure on the stock. Recent disclosures indicate that certain significant funds have notably reduced their positions in the company during the latest reporting period, suggesting a reallocation of capital that can affect stock valuations. The broader macroeconomic environment adds to market uncertainty. While the Federal Reserve maintained its current interest rate target in its recent meeting, it also acknowledged the uncertain implications of Middle East developments and upgraded its inflation projections, which could signal a more cautious outlook on future monetary policy. Moreover, recent US labor market data revealed a decline in nonfarm payroll employment, surpassing expectations, which may indicate a softening economic landscape that could eventually impact demand for semiconductors. Furthermore, the semiconductor industry's competitive landscape is evolving. An announcement was made by a prominent industry figure detailing plans for a new advanced semiconductor manufacturing project, which could introduce new competition in the high-end chip supply segment and prompt investors to re-evaluate long-term market concentration. title: Musk’s Terafab Plan Puts TSMC’s AI Chip Role In Focus - simplywall.st
content:
Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing, listed as NYSE:TSM, focuses on contract manufacturing of advanced chips for customers across AI, smartphones, data centers, and high performance computing. Musk's Terafab concept sits directly in these high end segments, where capacity and access to leading edge chips are core issues for large tech companies. For readers, this raises fresh questions about how concentrated the advanced foundry market will remain. Terafab is still at the plan stage, but the intent to supply Tesla, SpaceX, and xAI points to a more vertically integrated chip model than TSMC's foundry only approach. Investors following NYSE:TSM may want to watch how this development affects customer relationships, capital spending plans across the sector, and any new collaborations or rivalries that form around AI chip production capacity. Musk’s Terafab plan highlights how valuable advanced chip capacity has become, especially for AI and high-performance computing. It also underlines why TSMC’s pure-play foundry model still matters. Building a cutting-edge manufacturing network requires huge upfront capital, long lead times, and deep process know-how. That sets a high execution bar for any new entrant that wants to compete with TSMC, Samsung, or Intel on leading-edge nodes. For you as a TSMC watcher, Terafab is less about near-term earnings impact and more about how customers might think about securing capacity in the future, whether through long-term contracts with TSMC or by backing alternative suppliers and joint ventures. From here, focus on whether Terafab moves beyond concept into concrete milestones such as site selection, funding disclosures, and equipment orders, and whether names like TSMC, Samsung, or Intel appear as partners. Track any commentary from TSMC’s management on customer diversification, AI-focused capacity plans, and how it positions itself as a supplier to companies building their own AI hardware stacks. The balance between long-term, multi-year capacity agreements and shorter contracts will be important in judging how insulated TSMC remains from new entrants that want a bigger share of AI chip production. title: TSMC (TSM), Broadcom (AVGO), and Micron (MU): Three AI Infrastructure Plays Beyond Nvidia - MEXC
content: Three semiconductor industry players—Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing, Broadcom, and Micron—are capturing market attention as artificial intelligence infrastructure expansion accelerates. These companies occupy distinct positions within the supply chain enabling AI technology at scale. While Nvidia dominates media coverage, this trio provides essential components and capabilities that allow AI processors to function in production environments. Taiwan Semiconductor serves as the manufacturing partner for numerous leading chip architects, including Nvidia and AMD. The foundry giant announced in January that it anticipates 2026 revenue expansion of approximately 30% measured in US dollars, propelled by accelerating AI processor demand. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited, TSM Taiwan Semiconductor’s business model provides exposure across the entire AI ecosystem rather than dependency on any single designer. This diversified approach delivers growth regardless of which specific architectures gain market traction. Broadcom management recently identified TSMC’s manufacturing capacity as a constraint extending through 2026, underscoring the tightness in cutting-edge chip fabrication. Analyst sentiment leans decidedly positive. Among 15 analysts monitored by MarketBeat, 13 maintain bullish positions—comprising 10 buy and 3 strong buy ratings—alongside 2 hold recommendations and no sell ratings. Broadcom has established significant positioning in artificial intelligence through dual channels: customized processor design for cloud providers and networking equipment connecting AI compute clusters. Reuters coverage this month highlighted Broadcom’s projection for AI chip revenue surpassing $100 billion by 2027. This expansion stems from hyperscale cloud operators—massive infrastructure providers—developing proprietary AI accelerators rather than purchasing standard GPUs. Broadcom additionally provides switching and interconnect infrastructure required for operating large-scale AI facilities, creating revenue streams independent of processor sales alone. Analyst conviction runs deep. MarketBeat data reveals 33 total ratings, including 29 buy and 1 strong buy recommendations, against 3 hold positions and zero sell calls. The aggregate rating stands at “Moderate Buy.” Micron manufactures high-bandwidth memory, a technology now regarded as indispensable for AI servers and compute accelerators. Reuters reporting last week noted that Micron reported solid quarterly performance and issued revenue guidance substantially exceeding Wall Street consensus, with AI memory requirements serving as the primary catalyst. Micron represents one of merely three significant high-bandwidth memory suppliers worldwide, which constrains competitive pressure and underpins favorable pricing dynamics. Nevertheless, the company’s amplified capital investment roadmap generated some investor apprehension despite the impressive earnings performance. Analyst views remain overwhelmingly favorable. MarketBeat tracking shows 38 total ratings—29 buy and 5 strong buy recommendations—alongside 4 hold positions and zero sell ratings. Micron’s above-consensus revenue outlook provided the latest earnings-driven momentum for shares entering the current fiscal period. Taiwan Semiconductor, Broadcom, and Micron each command distinct territories within the AI infrastructure landscape, yet they currently share a common characteristic: robust analyst endorsement with universally absent sell recommendations. Whether this consensus persists as capital deployment intensifies and competitive dynamics evolve remains uncertain, but present data reflects a unified narrative across these three supply chain pillars.
